definition: A single conceptual change requires small edits in many different classes or files.
ruleOfThumb: If one feature request sends you on a repo-wide scavenger hunt, responsibilities are scattered.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: A new tax rule requires updating this system. What design problem does the team face?

options.text: One tax policy change requires editing many files, so the rule is scattered instead of centralized
options.explanation: The same business rule is duplicated across services and exports. Every policy update becomes multi-file surgery with high miss risk.

options.text: The problem is numeric precision; they should switch all tax calculations to bigint
options.explanation: Precision may matter, but this question highlights change-coupling across many locations, not numeric type choice.

options.text: The issue is that tax variables should use clearer names than taxA, taxB, taxC
options.explanation: Naming can improve readability, but it does not solve the scattered-change problem.

options.text: Each file should calculate tax independently so teams can work without coordination
options.explanation: Independent copies reduce short-term coordination but create long-term inconsistency and expensive updates.

questions.prompt: The company adds a new membership tier discount. Why is this codebase likely to produce inconsistent behavior?

options.text: Discount logic is duplicated across multiple modules, so adding a tier means synchronized edits everywhere and easy drift if one copy is missed
options.explanation: Scattered rules create inconsistent outcomes: checkout might show one discount while reports use another. Centralizing policy prevents divergence.

options.text: The real problem is that a ternary expression is less readable than an if statement
options.explanation: Syntax style is not the issue. The issue is identical policy logic duplicated in many places.

options.text: This design is acceptable because each class can optimize discount logic for its own context
options.explanation: These classes should not invent their own discount rules if they all represent the same business policy.

options.text: The code should use inheritance so all classes extend a BaseDiscountClass
options.explanation: Inheritance can still spread policy widely. The key fix is one source of truth, regardless of composition style.

questions.prompt: A legal rule update changes refund eligibility. Which version creates the bigger risk of missing one required edit?

options.text: Version B — one policy lives in several classes, so a legal update requires coordinated edits and is easy to apply incompletely
options.explanation: B turns one conceptual change into multi-location maintenance. A central policy class keeps changes atomic and auditable.

options.text: Version A — centralizing policy creates a single point of failure, so duplication is safer for compliance-heavy logic
options.explanation: For compliance, one authoritative policy is safer because it reduces mismatch risk and simplifies verification.
